---
title: "Caesar Weed (Urena lobata)"
slug: "urena-lobata"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622942658/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Caesar Weed"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Malvales"
tax_family: "Malvaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Malvales", "Malvaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622942658/medium.jpg" alt="Caesar Weed" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/sebento">
sebento</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342114333">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Malvales
-   **Family:** Malvaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=127638)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, fields, and forest edges.

## Natural history notes

A shrubby herb characterized by its soft, hairy leaves and pink to purple hibiscus-like flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, fields, and forest edges. It grows during the warmer months and produces bristly seed pods that readily attach to clothing or fur, aiding in dispersal, and can behave as a weedy or invasive species in suitable habitats.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/127638-Urena-lobata)
-   [Local profile](https://plant-directory.ifas.ufl.edu/plant-directory/urena-lobata/)
